data.title: China, Japan, S.Korea central bank deputies discussed US tariffs
data.description:
China, Japan and South Korea finance and central bank officials met and discussed the impact of U.S. tariffs on the global and regional macroeconomic situation, China's central bank said on Friday.

The People's Bank of China Deputy Governor Xuan Changneng was at the ASEAN and China, Japan and South Korea finance and central bank deputies meeting on April 8-9, according to a statement.

data.title: WiseTech Bull Moderates Outlook on Product DelayMarket Talk
data.description: The likelihood that WiseTech Global won't release its Container Transport Optimization product until late in the June half prompts its bull at Bell Potter to moderate his revenue forecasts. Analyst Chris Savage keeps a buy rating on the stock, but warns that there is even a chance that the logistics-software provider's new product will not be rolled out until the December half. He therefore takes a conservative view of the product's likely revenue contribution, which drives EPS forecast downgrades of 1% for fiscal 2025, 3% for fiscal 2026, and 4% for fiscal 2027. Bell Potter trims its target price 8.2% to A$112.50. Shares are down 2.1% at A$83.47. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: Iron Ore Prices Fall Amid Uncertainties Over U.S. TariffsMarket Talk
data.description: Iron ore prices are lower in early Asia trade, weighed by uncertainties surrounding U.S. tariffs. Global supply levels have fallen slightly while demand remains solid, Everbright Futures analysts write in a note. The current inventories have also fallen, while short-term prices are significantly affected by macroeconomic developments, they add. The analysts expect more near-term volatility. The most-traded iron ore contract on the Dalian Commodity Exchange is down 1.1% at CNY695.50 a ton. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

data.title: China's Xi to Visit Asean as Beijing Looks to Bolster Trade TiesMarket Talk
data.description: China is doubling down on efforts to firm trade ties with Asian neighbors with top-level foreign visits amid a deepening tariff war with the U.S. Chinese leader Xi Jinping will tour Southeast Asia next week, stopping by Vietnam, Malaysia and Cambodia, the Chinese foreign ministry has said. The high-stake visits come after a top-level meeting this week when the Chinese leadership pledged to deepen economic integration and strengthen supply-chain cooperation with the rest of the Asian region. Senior Chinese officials have also reached out to the European Union, Australia and Saudi Arabia, offering to "join hands" to cope with Trump tariffs. Analysts say Trump's tariff salvos may risk pushing American allies further into China's orbit. The upcoming visits will be Xi's first abroad this year. (singaporeeditors@dowjones.com)

data.title: Japan futures set for largest weekly drop since 2020 on US-Sino trade war
data.description:
• Japanese rubber futures pulled back on Friday, and were set for their largest weekly losses since late 2020, amid a widening trade war between the U.S. and top consumer China.

  • The Osaka Exchange (OSE) September rubber contract , fell 2.42% to 290.9 yen ($2.03) per kg as of 0201 GMT.

  • The contract has lost 9.29% so far this week, its largest weekly drop since December 7, 2020.

  • The May rubber contract on the Shanghai Futures Exchange (SHFE) dipped 0.17% to 14,705 yuan ($2,009.68) per metric ton.

  • The most active May butadiene rubber contract on the SHFE (SHBRv1) gained 0.7% to 11,555 yuan ($1,579.18) per metric ton.

  • U.S. President Donald Trump paused for 90 days most of the hefty duties he had imposed on dozens of countries, but boosted tariffs on Chinese imports to 145% from the 104% level that kicked in on Wednesday.

  • Earlier, Beijing announced an additional import levy on U.S. goods on Wednesday, imposing an 84% tariff.

  • The direct impact of tariffs on natural rubber is mainly in Chinese tire exports to the U.S., Chinese financial information site Tonghuashun Information said, adding that the expected demand for rubber is under pressure amid fears of a global recession.

  • China's car exports will likely face greater-than-forecast pressure this year as U.S. tariff hikes hit economies of its important overseas markets hard, a Chinese auto industry association said.

  • The dollar slid 0.9% to 143.10 yen , the weakest since October 1.

  • A stronger currency makes yen-denominated assets less affordable to overseas buyers.

  • The front-month rubber contract on the Singapore Exchange's SICOM platform for May delivery last traded at 164 U.S. cents per kg, down 1%.

($1 = 143.5100 yen)

($1 = 7.3171 Chinese yuan)

data.title: Tesla launches new Cybertruck variant in US, priced at $69,990
data.description:
Tesla on Thursday said it has launched a new version of Cybertruck in the United States, priced at $69,990.

The new Cybertruck long-range variant is the cheapest among the three models it currently has in the U.S., Tesla's website shows.

data.title: Vietnam's VN Index Opens Flat at 1168.34
Tesla Launches Cybertruck Long Range In The US, Priced At $69,990 - Website

data.title: Hero MotoCorp Forms Wholly-Owned Subsidiary in Brazil
data.description:
Indian two-wheeler manufacturer Hero MotoCorp has incorporated a wholly-owned subsidiary in Brazil named Hero MotoCorp Do Brasil, according to a Thursday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

The Brazilian subsidiary will deal in the manufacturing and distribution of two-wheeler vehicles as well as parts and accessories.

data.title: Jade Gas advances Red Lake development in Mongolia with second successful well
data.description: Jade Gas remains on target to fast-track development of its Red Lake gas field in the South Gobi region of Mongolia after further drilling success. Jade has identified a high-value gas opportunity in Mongolia and is on track to quickly supply local buyers from what it considers a world-class asset. The Red Lake field sits in the Tavan Tolgoi Basin, a region with high demand for electricity, fuel and the base load energy supply necessary to underpin the region’s intermittent renewables. Future gas producer Announcing the successful drilling of a second production well at Red Lake, executive chair Dennis Morton said the results confirmed Jade’s confidence in the field being a future world-class gas producer. “This is a wonderful situation for the company as all gas sales options target high energy replacement costs,” Mr Morton said. “This second well outshines the first well with higher gas readings supporting the company’s desire to quickly commercialise the gas [and] we will now look to put the two into production within the next few weeks and move on to drilling the next wells.” Commercial pathway Jade now expects to complete an agreement with a midstream manufacturer of specialist compressed natural gas (CNG) equipment, which will enable the company to commercialise the gas from these initial wells. “The CNG facilities are easily transportable and are able to take lightly processed gas and convert it into a saleable and marketable form, enabling the company to potentially deliver to various markets in the immediate vicinity,” Mr Morton said. “Our gas commercialisation plan is built around a modular and scalable LNG project but we are also canvassing other nearer-term and lower-capex opportunities in the form of CNG, a product that is also utilised in displacing diesel in coal haulage trucks and which we expect will be in great demand,” Mr Morton said. “These markets are in need of a cleaner and more secure fuel source, including the displacement of diesel which currently powers the large coal haulage truck fleet in the South Gobi.”

data.title: Bitcoin Holds The Line—But Can It Bounce Back or Break Lower?
data.description:
Bitcoin price started a fresh increase above the $80,000 zone. BTC is now correcting gains and might struggle to stay above the $79,500 support.

  • Bitcoin started a fresh increase above the $80,000 zone.
  • The price is trading above $79,500 and the 100 hourly Simple moving average.
  • There is a new connecting bearish trend line forming with resistance at $80,500 on the hourly chart of the BTC/USD pair (data feed from Kraken).
  • The pair could start another increase if it clears the $80,500 zone.

Bitcoin Price Dips Again

Bitcoin price started a fresh increase above the $77,500 zone. BTC formed a base and gained pace for a move above the $79,500 and $80,000 resistance levels.

The bulls pumped the price above the $82,500 resistance. A high was formed at $83,548 and the price recently started a downside correction. There was a move below the $81,500 support. The price dipped below the 23.6% Fib retracement level of the upward move from the $74,572 swing low to the $83,548 high.

Bitcoin price is now trading above $79,200 and the 100 hourly Simple moving average. On the upside, immediate resistance is near the $80,500 level. There is also a new connecting bearish trend line forming with resistance at $80,500 on the hourly chart of the BTC/USD pair.

The first key resistance is near the $81,500 level. The next key resistance could be $82,500. A close above the $82,500 resistance might send the price further higher. In the stated case, the price could rise and test the $83,500 resistance level. Any more gains might send the price toward the $85,000 level.

More Losses In BTC?

If Bitcoin fails to rise above the $80,500 resistance zone, it could continue to move down. Immediate support on the downside is near the $79,500 level. The first major support is near the $79,000 level and the 50% Fib retracement level of the upward move from the $74,572 swing low to the $83,548 high.

The next support is now near the $78,000 zone. Any more losses might send the price toward the $76,500 support in the near term. The main support sits at $75,000.

Technical indicators:

Hourly MACD – The MACD is now losing pace in the bullish zone.

Hourly RSI (Relative Strength Index) – The RSI for BTC/USD is now above the 50 level.

Major Support Levels – $79,500, followed by $79,000.

Major Resistance Levels – $80,500 and $81,500.

data.title: Malaysia Palm Oil Outlook Likely to Stay Positive Despite U.S. TariffsMarket Talk
data.description: U.S. tariffs are likely to help palm oil demand in Malaysia in the medium term, especially if the U.S. shifts imports of palm oil from Indonesia on lower tariffs, RHB analysts say in a note. Before announcing a 90-day pause on the so-called reciprocal tariffs on Wednesday, the U.S. briefly imposed a 32% tariff on imports from Indonesia and 24% on goods from Malaysia. Demand could also rise if China substitutes U.S. soybeans with palm oil, following Beijing's 84% retaliatory tariffs on U.S. products, they say. Palm oil demand is expected to pick up in April as pricing becomes more attractive compared to soybean oil, they add. RHB maintains an overweight rating on the region's plantation sector, naming Johor Plantations, Sarawak Oil Palms, Bumitama Agri, London Sumatra Indonesia and SD Guthrie as its top picks. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: Mitsubishi Corporation makes DennoKotsu an equity-method affiliate company, strengthening efforts to address regional transportation challenges
data.description:
TOKYO, Apr 11, 2025 - (JCN Newswire) - Mitsubishi Corporation (MC) is pleased to announce its participation in a Series D round of funding, involving a third-party allotment of shares underwritten by 12 companies, conducted by DennoKotsu Inc. As a result of this acquisition, DennoKotsu, which promotes the digital transformation of taxi dispatch operations, will become an equity-method affiliate of MC.

In cooperation with local governments and transportation companies, MC has been working to address regional transportation challenges in Japan through AI-equipped on-demand bus and autonomous driving projects. MC previously made investments in DennoKotsu in 2020 and 2023, which have helped promote the digital transformation of its taxi dispatch business and call center services. By leveraging DennoKotsu's achievements, expertise, and practical knowledge in the taxi industry, along with MC's extensive business network, cooperation with other MC-affiliated companies has already begun. DennoKotsu's dispatch system is currently in use in 47 prefectures across Japan, supporting approximately 600 companies and 21,000 vehicles.

MC believes that automating transportation is a key solution to Japan's growing driver shortage. In anticipation of autonomous vehicles becoming more prevalent, DennoKotsu is expected to expand its operations beyond the taxi sector to the broader transportation field. This expectation led to MC's additional funding for DennoKotsu. With this investment, MC aims to further strengthen its collaboration with DennoKotsu and accelerate cooperation with on-demand bus services and autonomous driving projects. Through this, the company will provide comprehensive solutions tailored to the needs of each region, advancing transportation problem-solving and the creation of new businesses.

Inquiry Recipient:

Mitsubishi Corporation

Telephone:+81-3-3210-2171

Source: Mitsubishi Corporation

Copyright 2025 JCN Newswire . All rights reserved.

data.title: PBOC, BoJ and BoK met to discuss the impact of Trump tariffs on the global economy
data.description:
This readout via the People's Bank of China:

  • China’s Xuan Changneng, Deputy Governor of the PBoC, attended the ASEAN+3 Finance and Central Bank Deputies Meeting in Kuala Lumpur.

  • He chaired discussions as China’s co-chair of the 10+3 Finance and Financial Cooperation Mechanism.

  • Key topics included:

    • Impact of US tariffs on global and regional macroeconomics.

    • Strengthening the Chiang Mai Initiative Multilateralization (CMIM).

    • Enhancing the role of the 10+3 Macroeconomic Research Office (AMRO).

    • Deepening regional policy coordination and improving the regional financial safety net.

  • The meeting adopted legal arrangements for RMB investment under the CMIM.

  • China, Japan, and South Korea also held a separate trilateral meeting to discuss economic and financial cooperation.

  • Xuan reaffirmed China’s commitment to a moderately loose monetary policy to support market stability and economic recovery.

  • He held bilateral talks with senior officials from South Korea and Singapore to discuss global uncertainty and its national impacts.

Bolding above is mine.

data.title: VHM Gets Green Light for Victoria Rare Earth and Mineral Sands Project; Shares Rise 7%
data.description:
VHM said Resources Victoria approved a mining license for its Goschen rare earth and mineral sands project, according to a Friday Australian bourse filing.

It will be entitled to mine the land covered by the license, explore for minerals, and construct mining facilities related to the mining operation.

The first production at Goschen is anticipated for late 2026. All necessary remaining secondary approvals to construct, operate, and close the project are progressing and the firm will also finalize investment talks and develop strategic partnerships.

It recently appointed an independent technical, environmental, and social due diligence review for the project financing.

Its shares rose 7% in recent trading on Friday.

data.title: South Korean Shares Down at Friday's Open Amid Escalated Trade War
data.description:
Southe Korean shares opened lower on Friday, mirroring regional declines as investor sentiment soured following the Nasdaq's slump and heightened U.S.-China trade friction.

The Korea Composite Stock Price Index or Kospi opened 44 points or 1.8% lower at 2,400.74 before retracing its decline to trade 40.11 points, or nearly 1.6% lower at 2,406.21 at 10:58 am KST. The Kosdaq shed 0.20 points, or about 0.03%, to 681.60 at 10:58 am KST.

As a tech-heavy market vulnerable to global risk appetite, the Kospi faced selling pressure amid growing concerns about monetary tightening and corporate earnings uncertainty.

Meanwhile, trade tensions between the two largest economies heightened as the White House clarified that tariffs on Chinese imports is now at least 145%, up from 125% disclosed Wednesday.

data.title: ST Engineering Awarded Turnkey Rail Services Contract
data.description:
Singapore Technologies Engineering's Urban Solutions business was awarded a turnkey rail services contract for a new Taichung MRT Blue Line worth SG$1.4 billion by the Rapid Transit System Bureau of Taichung City Government, Taiwan.

The contract will span 14 years and is expected to start during the second quarter of the year, according to a company release on Thursday.

Under the contract, the company will lead the project management, systems integration and provide critical elements to the development of an above-ground train depot.

data.title: London metals mixed on Trump's tariff pause
data.description:
Base metals in London traded mixed on Friday and were headed for a weekly rise after U.S. President Donald Trump decided to temporarily lower the hefty duties for dozens of countries and the dollar weakened.

The benchmark three-month copper on the London Metal Exchange (LME) was steady at $8,985 per metric ton as of 0130 GMT. It has gained 2.3% so far this week.

The most-traded copper contract on the Shanghai Futures Exchange (SHFE) rose 0.9% to 74,560 yuan per metric ton. It has lost 5.8% from Thursday's close of 79,190 yuan per ton. SHFE was closed on April 4 for a holiday.

The dollar slumped as waning confidence in the U.S. economy prompted investors to ditch U.S. assets to the benefit of safe havens.

A softer dollar makes greenback-priced commodities cheaper for buyers using other currencies.

Trump on Wednesday announced a 90-day pause on tariffs for most countries in a stunning U-turn after a days-long market rout that erased trillions of dollars from global stocks.

However, the U.S. president ratcheted up duties on Chinese imports to an effective 145% rate on Thursday, further escalating a high-stakes confrontation between the world's two largest economies.

"Market uncertainty persists, largely driven by the unpredictable nature of trade policies and tariffs. As a result, investors remain cautious, with ongoing concerns about the potential for a recession due to the ongoing trade tensions," a base metals trader said.

SHFE aluminium rose 0.5% to 19,705 yuan a ton, zinc added 0.8% to 22,560 yuan, lead gained 0.6% to 16,740 yuan, nickel was up 1.1% at 120,350 yuan, tin advanced 1.7% to 252,350 yuan.

LME aluminium lost 0.1% to $2,368 a ton, lead rose 0.1% to $1,893.5, tin was up 0.1% at $30,685, zinc CMZN3 fell 0.3% to $2,632 and nickel shed 0.4% to $14,740 a ton.

data.title: As Trump's trade war rages, the shipping industry wrestles with uncertainty and the risk of disruption
data.description:
By James Rogers

The Trump administration's rapid escalation of tariffs 'may be reshaping global trade flows,' says logistics-tech company Descartes

President Donald Trump's wide-ranging tariffs have sent shock waves through America's trade partners and are already being felt in the complex supply chains that transport goods around the world.

Simon Sundboell, chief executive of Copenhagen, Denmark-based maritime-intelligence company eeSea, told MarketWatch that uncertainty over tariffs is impacting the shipping industry. Set against this backdrop, eeSea is seeing an increase in so-called blank sailings, where shipping carriers cancel or delay their sailings.

"We're seeing more canceled sailings - not yet an alarming level, but we certainly had an uptick compared to what we would see in the same period last year, and an increased number of cancellations announced in the last weeks," he said.

Related: Trump's tariff war is tanking shipping stocks-but this one stands out for its lack of exposure

These cancellations, which are primarily in sailings from Asia to North America, will hit the market in April, May and June, according to Sundboell.

The CEO said that a host of companies involved in the global supply chain will be closely monitoring the situation, from retail and consumer-product giants such as Walmart Inc. (WMT) and Procter & Gamble Co. (PG), to shipping companies such as Maersk (DK:MAERSK.A) (DK:MAERSK.B), Matson Inc. (MATX) and Chinese company Cosco Shipping Holdings Co. Ltd. (CICOY) (HK:1919) (CN:601919), among others.

"Everybody will be making scenarios right now, whether you're a cargo owner like Walmart or Procter & Gamble, or you're an ocean carrier like Mediterranean Shipping Company or Cosco, or anywhere else in the supply chain like ports, rail operators and freight forwarders," Sundboell said. "All of these will be making best-case and worst-case scenarios right now."

Related: Trump's China tariffs are a 'Category 5 price storm' for U.S. consumers, warns analyst Dan Ives

Shipping stocks have already taken a hit from Trump's aggressive tariff agenda. The tariffs have also been described as a "Category 5 price storm" for U.S. consumers.

Despite all the uncertainty, U.S. container imports performed well in March, increasing 6.3% over February and 11% year over year, according to a report released Thursday by logistics-technology company Descartes.

The strong numbers could signal stockpiling efforts as the Trump tariffs loomed. March's total of 2,380,674 TEUs, or "20-foot-equivalent container units," marked the third-highest volume ever recorded for the month, according to Descartes, trailing only March 2022 and March 2021.

Opinion: Trump caved on tariffs. Here's how he can really put America first.

U.S. imports from China totaled 762,830 TEUs in March, a 12.6% decline from February, but a 9.4 % increase over the same month last year.

"While March volumes remain well below the July 2024 peak of 1,022,913 TEUs, China continues to be the U.S.'s top maritime trade partner, though escalating tariffs could disrupt volumes in the months ahead," wrote Jackson Wood, Descartes' director of industry strategy and global trade intelligence, in the report.

The global tariffs landscape is shifting rapidly. On Wednesday, Trump paused his so-called reciprocal tariffs on all countries for 90 days. However, China was excluded from this and was hit with greater tariffs of 125% - and on Wednesday, the White House said that China's new tariff rate had been revised further upward, to 145%.

The U.S. administration's rapid escalation of tariffs "may be reshaping global trade flows and are prompting U.S. importers to re-evaluate sourcing strategies," according to Descartes' Wood. He also pointed to shifting port dynamics, such as East Coast and Gulf Coast ports overtaking West Coast ports, and ongoing instability in the Middle East and Eastern Europe.

"As the logistics environment grows more unpredictable, businesses face heightened risks of disruption," Wood added.

-James Rogers

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Dollar Languishes Near 3-Year Low
data.description:
The U.S. dollar index fell to around 100 on Friday, approaching its lowest level in three years as economic concerns and fading confidence in U.S. assets continued to weigh on the currency.

Investors remain wary of the potential fallout from President Trump’s tariff policies, which have raised fears of slower economic growth despite a 90-day reprieve meant to allow for trade negotiations.

Trump’s move to raise tariffs on Chinese imports to 145% has escalated the trade war with Beijing, further denting sentiment.

Meanwhile, the European Union has paused the implementation of countermeasures for 90 days in a bid to pursue talks.

Adding to pressure on the dollar, U.S. core CPI rose just 2.8% year-over-year — the slowest pace since March 2021 — reinforcing expectations of a dovish Federal Reserve.

The greenback weakened broadly, with the steepest losses seen against the euro, Japanese yen and Swiss franc.

data.title: South Korean Won Rises on Weaker Dollar
data.description:
The South Korean won strengthened around 1,450 per dollar as the greenback weakened amid mounting concerns over escalating US-China trade tensions.

Despite the US granting a 90-day grace period on reciprocal tariffs, uncertainties persist due to the rising tariff dispute, with new White House measures pushing the effective tariff rate on Chinese imports to a staggering 145%.

In response, South Korea’s Finance Minister called for contingency plans to address uncertainties from US tariff policies and enhance financial market monitoring.

Meanwhile, early data showed South Korea's exports rose 13.7% year-on-year in the first 10 days of April, driven by strong demand for chips and automobiles, boosting sentiment for the export-reliant won.

By destination, shipments to China climbed 8.8%, while those to the US fell by 0.6%.

Adding to the momentum, Trump stated that the US may buy advanced ships from close allies, a key area of cooperation between South Korea and the US.

data.title: Ringgit Likely to Remain Resilient Amid Trade War UncertaintyMarket Talk
data.description: The Malaysian ringgit is likely to remain resilient next week, supported by the reciprocal tariff delay and weaker dollar sentiment, Kenanga IB economists say in a note. However, the currency outlook could remain cautious as global trade tensions persist, especially with the 145% U.S. tariff on China and a broader 10% global levy, they warn. Upcoming data from China, U.S. retail sales and the ECB decision may drive forex moves, they note. Technically, the pair's outlook appears neutral and could hover around 4.463, they add. Kenanga pegs USD/MYR's short-term resistance at 4.478 and support at 4.421. USD/MYR is 0.6% lower at 4.4400.(yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: Indonesia's Jakarta Composite Index Opens 0.9% Lower at 6195.57
Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0210 GMT - Chinese shares are lower in early trade as the U.S.-China trade tensions escalate. The tariffs imposed on China by President Trump in his second term add up to 145% and not 125%, the White House said Thursday. Investors are watching for any response by Beijing and potential stimulus measures to offset the drag on exports. Home appliance makers are leading the declines. Midea Group falls 1.6% and Gree Electric Appliances sheds 1.1%. Consumer stocks are broadly lower, with Yonghui Superstores down 2.25%. The benchmark Shanghai Composite Index is down 0.1% at 3221.21, the Shenzhen Composite Index is off 0.1%, and the ChiNext Price Index is 0.4% lower. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0203 GMT - Malaysia's oil-and-gas sector outlook may be weighed by lower oil prices, amid concerns over U.S. reciprocal tariffs and OPEC+ output hikes, Hong Leong IB analyst Brian Chin says in a note. He turns cautious on upstream exploration and production names like Hibiscus Petroleum due to earnings sensitivity to Brent prices, and expects oil majors to delay new capital expenditure. He favors local maintenance service providers like Dayang Enterprise and large caps like Dialog and MISC, which are more resilient in volatile times. Petrochemical prices could also face downside risks from lower oil prices and demand concerns, he says. Hong Leong maintains a neutral rating on the sector, and pegs Dialog Group and MISC as its sector's top picks. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0203 GMT - Hong Kong shares open lower, tracking overnight losses on Wall Street. The Hang Seng Index drops 0.6% to 20585.05 and the Hang Seng Tech Index edges up 0.1%. Investors are closely watching for any developments related to U.S.-China trade tensions. The White House clarified on Thursday that tariffs imposed on China actually stands at 145%. Among major stocks, industrial and consumer goods stocks lead the declines, with Techtronic Industries dropping 4.4% and Shenzhen International Group 3.0% lower. Gainers include Alibaba Health Information Technology, which is 3.8% higher, and Geely Automobile Holdings, which gains 2.7%.(tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0126 GMT - Taiwan stocks open lower after sharp gains on Thursday. Although investor sentiment was boosted by President Trump's 90-day tariff pause, ING economist Lynn Song says that it is crucial for Taiwan to secure a trade deal with the U.S. over the next 90 days. A failure to do so would pose a notable risk to Taiwan's trade and growth outlook in 2H, he says in a note. TSMC drops 2.0% and Foxconn Technology is down 3.2%. Quanta Computer falls 0.5% and MediaTek is 0.8% lower. The Taiex index is 1.9% lower at 18638.67. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0110 GMT - Malaysian palm oil stocks could rise 7% on month in April, driven by stronger production and softer domestic demand, CIMB Securities analysts Ivy Ng Lee Fang and Lim Yue Jia say in a note. Escalating U.S.-China trade tensions and falling crude oil prices could weigh on near-term CPO prices and commodity demand, they warn. They expect planters to report sequentially weaker 1Q earnings amid lower output and softer CPO prices. CIMB maintains its 2025 CPO price forecast at MYR4,200/ton but expects weaker prices in 2Q amid rising supply. It maintains an overweight rating on Malaysia's agriculture & forestry sector, pegging SD Guthrie and IOI Corp. as its top sector picks. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0107 GMT - Singapore's FTSE Straits Times Index falls 3.0% to 3469.99 in early trade, tracking Wall Street's tumble overnight. The main theme was risk-off in another volatile session on Thursday, Commerzbank Research analysts say in a report. "Trade tensions with China reignited investor anxiety," they say, noting the White House's clarification that tariffs on China are now 145%. Among the worst performers on the benchmark index, SATS Ltd. loses 5.3%, Seatrium drops 4.6%, and index heavyweight DBS sheds 4.5%. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0106 GMT - Malaysia's benchmark Kuala Lumpur Composite Index is 1.7% lower at 1437.72. The local market sees profit-taking after Wall Street's overnight drop, with tariff concerns weighing on sentiment, especially in tech, Apex Securities says in a note. Volatility is expected as investors await clearer signals from the U.S. PPI data due later, it says. Given global uncertainties, the brokerage prefers defensive sectors like REITs, utilities, and healthcare while trimming tech exposure. Among the decliners, CIMB Group is down 3.8% and Gamuda Bhd. is 4.2% lower. Meanwhile, Telekom Malaysia adds 1.1% and QL Resources is up 1.7%. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0016 GMT - Japanese stocks are lower following Thursday's surge and as growing trade tensions between the U.S. and China weigh on the global economic outlook. Auto, electronics and financial stocks are leading declines. Nissan Motor is down 7.0%, Hitachi Ltd. is 6.0% lower and Sumitomo Mitsui Financial Group is down 6.1%. USD/JPY is at 143.39, down sharply from 146.78 as of Thursday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are closely watching any developments related to U.S. tariffs. The Nikkei Stock Average is down 5.1% at 32831.20, following Thursday's 9.1% rise. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

2345 GMT - Japanese stocks may fall following Thursday's surge and as growing trade tensions between the U.S. and China raise uncertainty over the global economic outlook. Nikkei futures are down 4.2% at 33225 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 143.82, down sharply from 146.78 as of Thursday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any policy steps to be taken by governments in Asia in response to U.S. tariffs. The Nikkei Stock Average rose 9.1% to 34609.00 on Thursday, its biggest percentage gain since Aug. 6. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

2257 GMT [Dow Jones]--Uranium producer Boss Energy is very well placed to beat FY2025 production guidance of 850,000 pounds of U3O8 after reporting drumming 295,800 pounds at the Honeymoon mine in 3Q, Macquarie analysts say in a note. "This is despite some minor challenges with the kiln and baghouse resulting in some downtime," the analysts say. They now expect FY2025 output of 912,000 pounds. Macquarie has an outperform rating and A$4.60 target on Boss. The analysts say it "offers strong leverage to a recovery in uranium spot prices and improving term structures." Boss last traded at A$2.62. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com)

2225 GMT - Lovesac shares were among those hardest hit in the new tariff world given its exposure to Vietnam, one of the countries most affected by President Trump's tariff plan. Trump on Wednesday afternoon announced a 90-day-pause to some tariffs, allowing Lovesac to bounce back on its Vietnam exposure becoming less of a liability than thought. But it won't be immune to the trade war with China, responsible to around 13% of its products costs, DA Davidson analysts say in a research note. DA Davidson cuts its target price to $24 a share from $35 a share previously. Lovesac closed up 16% to $18.49.(sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

2216 GMT - Australian stocks look set to continue their whipsaw week, with futures pointing to a sharp opening reversal. ASX futures are down by 1.5% ahead of Friday's session, suggesting that the benchmark S&P/ASX 200 index will give back a chunk of the 4.5% gain it compiled on Thursday. That was its largest one-day advance since 2020. With the Trump administration's trade tariffs roiling global markets, the ASX 200 also logged its largest decline since 2020 this week. U.S. equities provided a soft lead after staging their own reversal. The DJIA lost 2.5%, the S&P 500 fell 3.5%, and the Nasdaq Composite tumbled 4.3%. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: Chinese Shares Fall as Trade Tensions GrowMarket Talk
data.description: Chinese shares are lower in early trade as the U.S.-China trade tensions escalate. The tariffs imposed on China by President Trump in his second term add up to 145% and not 125%, the White House said Thursday. Investors are watching for any response by Beijing and potential stimulus measures to offset the drag on exports. Home appliance makers are leading the declines. Midea Group falls 1.6% and Gree Electric Appliances sheds 1.1%. Consumer stocks are broadly lower, with Yonghui Superstores down 2.25%. The benchmark Shanghai Composite Index is down 0.1% at 3221.21, the Shenzhen Composite Index is off 0.1%, and the ChiNext Price Index is 0.4% lower. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

data.title: Dollar slides to decade-low vs Swiss franc as US assets hit by loss of confidence
data.description:
Key points:
  • Analysts see loss of confidence in U.S. assets on Trump trade policies
  • Euro leaps to three-year peak, gold marks fresh all-time high
  • Yen hits multi-month high after selloff in U.S. shares, Treasuries
  • Yuan strengthens despite escalating U.S. trade war with China

The dollar slumped on Friday as waning confidence in the U.S. economy prompted investors to ditch U.S. assets to the benefit of safe havens such as the Swiss franc, yen and euro, as well as gold.

The yellow metal recorded a new all-time peak, and the franc notched a fresh decade high.

Investors dumped Wall Street stocks overnight, as a powerful relief rally on Wednesday - when President Donald Trump abruptly paused higher tariff rates on dozens of trading partners - reversed course in a frenetic 24-hour period for markets. Longer-dated U.S. Treasuries are also selling off, putting 10-year yields on course for their biggest weekly jump since 2001.

Trump's 90-day respite, which came despite his insistence for days that his policies would never change, didn't include China. Instead, he ratcheted up duties on Chinese imports to an effective 145% rate, further escalating a high-stakes confrontation between the world's two largest economies.

The Chinese yuan had tumbled to an all-time low in offshore trading on Tuesday, but erased all those losses a day later, and surged again on Thursday. It initially strengthened in the latest session as well, before trading slightly weaker.

"There has been a pronounced 'sell U.S.' vibe flowing through broad markets and into the classic safe-haven assets, with the USD losing the safe-haven bid," said Chris Weston, head of research at Pepperstone.

"The moves (have) the feel of repatriation flows by foreign entities, with many re-focused on the idea that Trump's reluctant pause on tariffs was due to increased system risk, and migrating capital away from Ground Zero."

U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent asserted on Wednesday that the tariff pullback had been the plan all along to bring countries to the bargaining table. Trump, though, later indicated that the near-panic in markets that had unfolded since his April 2 "Liberation Day" tariff announcements had factored into his thinking.

Since returning to the White House in January, Trump has repeatedly threatened an array of punitive measures on trading partners, only to revoke some of them at the last minute. The on-again, off-again approach has baffled world leaders and spooked business executives, who say the uncertainty has made it difficult to forecast market conditions.

The dollar dropped as much as 1.2% to 0.81405 Swiss franc for the first time since January 2015, extending Thursday's nearly 4% plunge.

The U.S. currency slid 1.1% to 142.88 yen , the weakest since September 30.

It also slumped 0.5% to a five-month trough at C$1.3910 .

The euro surged as much as 1.7% to $1.13855, a level last seen in February 2022.

The dollar index , which measures the greenback against those four currencies and two more major counterparts, sagged as much as 1.2%, taking it below the 100 level for the first time since July 2023.

The U.S. currency drooped as much as 0.3% to 7.2903 yuan in the offshore market in early trading, but was last up 0.1% to 7.3211. In the previous two sessions, it slid 1.5%.

Gold jumped 1.4% to an unprecedented $3,219.23 per ounce.

Elsewhere, the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield climbed nearly 10 basis points early on Friday to 4.488%.

"Trump blinked on higher yields, but that doesn't mean 10-year yields can't retest 4.50% while the USD sells off in tandem," said Brent Donnelly, president of Spectra Markets.

"I think we are entering a pure 'sell USD' regime," he said. "Rate differentials are losing their sway over the USD for the first time in my life."

data.title: Market Chatter: China Goods to US Face Minimum 145% Tariff
data.description:
Chinese goods now face a minimum tariff of 145% in the US, after President Donald Trump again raised tariffs on China after Beijing took retaliatory actions against previous levies, The New York Times reported Thursday.

The White House said the recent 125% tariff increase was in addition to an existing 20% levy on Chinese goods related to fentanyl supply, according to the report.

Goods already in transit are exempted from the new tariffs, the NYT wrote.

Disclaimer

Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.

data.title: Japan Ready to Talk Forex, Non-Tariff Issues in U.S. Trade Deals
data.description:
Japan is prepared to discuss non-tariff issues, including exchange rates, with the Trump administration to secure an exemption from higher U.S. tariffs, Economy Minister Ryosei Akazawa said on Friday.

Having been appointed to lead negotiations with Washington, he added, "As Secretary Bessent has reportedly mentioned non-tariff barriers and exchange rates, we would of course be open to discussions if such topics come up." Akazawa plans to visit the U.S. soon to build trust but expects tough negotiations, given Bessent’s financial expertise.

Calling the tariffs a "national crisis," the minister vowed to prioritize Tokyo’s objective, saying, "The most and absolutely important thing is our national interest, so we will proceed based on that view."
